raw_survey_responses.preparedness_feedback: Supportive team that AISM owned
Hard to know as we havent really started until Monday. Key issue was using on line tools and systems that our kids have never used before which added stress.
Most useful was the parent handbook although a simple checklist of "who to contact in case of" could have been at the forefront of info for parents.
update information
Putting new parents on a WhatsApp group. Other parents have been there from much earlier and don't share the same concerns or have already gone through them in previous years
a map of the campus as it caused a lot of "first day stress" with the boys not being shown around and therefore not knowing where to go

A daily lesson schedule and more clarity around how lunch ordering (or bringing your own) is organised would've been good. I ended up organising my own transport, as I didn't get follow-up soon enough from school representatives. Thankfully I got the contact from my colleague. This would be a problem if I hadn't have known anyone.   It would be helpful if the student's preferred name was the one used in email addresses, engage, etc., as this is very confusing for both student's and teachers trying to locate the students and helping my children sign in to email and school-based apps. The technology set-up for new students wasn't comprehensive enough in the right area - one of my children still can't sign in to Engage, and another child was on guest wifi for the first 7 weeks and couldn't work out why nothing was working. The session provided for new students in the MSS was very lengthy, but it would have been better if it was tailored just for access, not actually the 'how to use' apps component (which the teachers can do). If the kids aren't confident with how to sign in to email and Engage, then nothing else works. This should be the focus of the technology induction.
One hour trail class for kids could give them idea
The timetable
orientation by subject teachers would have been most welcome.

SeeSaw, music class arrangement, additional/after school activities. On top of it, it was mentioned during the orientation that first time purchase of school uniforms will enjoy discount but it was not given. School might need to be more accurate when giving out such information.
The queueing system needed for child pickup

Due to the pandemic, the school uniforms can only be ordered online, that creates a lot inaccuracy in the size measurement, its such a hassle for us to travel to the outlet for exchange and view it just to ensure the size fits. Perhaps the school can get the supplier to provide uniforms samples in various sizes, to ease the parents to place order or exchange at the campus.
We have adequate essential information and guidance in preparing our kids to starting school. All good!!!
orientation day is good platform for us to get to know the school , environment and also expectations
Timetable provided ,
Syllabus of each subjects, so when we got free time, we can study and do revision with our children, thank you
Information from the Principal and admin office was very helpful
Curriculum
Navien was most informative. We were well prepared to start.
Being able to easily contact the teacher online.
Induction
Orientation day for new students and parents
nothing now

1. Our experience of purchasing school uniforms was unpleasant. School uniforms size chart is inaccurate that wasted time on communication with suppliers and many trips to Tek Bookmart for exchange and return. There should be a set of samples provided by the supplier, even if they are not for trying out. At least parents can see if that size fits their kid since size chart are wrong.  2. Bus transporter is not clear with the pickup point that our son has to cross a very busy road on his own on his 1st day of school. As parents, safety of the children is our main concern.
Orientation session
New Parent ICT information pack
Reports from other schools, therapist reports, development specialist reports all are good tools to use when understanding a child better before he/she starts school
Visiting the school
The information when actually observing and experiencing. School visit tours and trial class.

raw_survey_responses.preparedness_feedback: Information about their teachers, being linked in with other parents in the school so we could have connected our children with peers prior to school starting. We received an informal “tour” of the school on the parent orientation day, at our request. A more formal transition/induction day for the children would have been really helpful for them. The session we attended was information for parents, not so much the kids themselves. At previous schools we have received a book (social story) for the kids explaining where their classrooms were, who their teachers are, where the canteen and library is, where they can play, information about the uniforms etc. This was a great way for them to become familiar with the school prior to their arrival. Other transitions we’ve experienced included a “meet the teacher” session for 10minutes the day before school officially started so they could build a relationship with them, see where their classroom was etc. When this was not possible we had a video introduction the teacher filmed and sent to us. We also had a school where the teacher posted a letter to the kids over the holidays introducing themselves and welcoming them to the school. These are just some examples, but they have all helped our kids transition into new schools on previous occasions.
raw_survey_responses.recommendation_feedback: A more formal, supported transition process.

I felt very reassured knowing that I could easily get in touch through WhatsApp.

Whenever I had a question, I was able to get the information I needed right away, so there were no issues.
raw_survey_responses.recommendation_feedback:
I believe we are living in a time where academic performance and entrance to prestigious schools are no longer the only goals that define success — and many parents around the world are starting to feel the same.
What makes AISM stand out, and why I chose it, is the strong focus not only on academics but also on values, wellbeing, and personal growth. Our children have grown up with a strong emphasis on SEL (Social Emotional Learning) in Japan, and I felt AISM was a perfect match because those elements are truly embedded in the school culture.
To make AISM a school that more families would want to recommend, I believe it’s important to continue sharing this holistic educational approach clearly and consistently — for example, through SMS communications, school events, and community engagement.
Knowing that there are multiple paths and choices in life helps children feel secure and empowered. If AISM continues to celebrate diversity in learning and success, it will become even more attractive to families looking for a school that nurtures the whole child.
